title: Should the US Ban Smartphones in K-12 Public Schools?
prompt: Evaluate whether US public K-12 schools should be required to ban student smartphone use during the school day (bell-to-bell). Consider adolescent mental health data, attention and learning outcomes, equity and safety concerns, parent communication needs, enforcement realities, and the experience of districts and countries that have already implemented bans.

agents.bio: Clinical researcher who studies teen anxiety, depression, and screen time. Tracks the post-2012 mental health curves and Jonathan Haidt's body of work closely. Believes the correlational evidence linking phone-based childhood to adolescent harm is strong enough to act on without waiting for perfect causal proof.

Runs a 1,400-student public high school in a mid-sized district that piloted a Yondr-pouch policy two years ago. Has firsthand data on referrals, fights, tardies, and teacher retention before and after. Cares about what is actually enforceable on a Tuesday afternoon, not what sounds good in op-eds.

ACLU-affiliated attorney focused on student speech, search, and surveillance cases. Worries that confiscation policies create Fourth Amendment problems, disproportionately punish low-income and minority students, and centralize power in administrators with weak due process. Skeptical of moral panics dressed up as policy.

Parent of two middle schoolers in a district that experienced a lockdown last year. Texted her kids during the event and credits the phone for her sanity. Believes the post-Uvalde reality means parents will never accept losing real-time contact with their children during school hours, regardless of what researchers say.

Quantitative ed-policy researcher who has reviewed the Norwegian, French, and UK ban studies. Distinguishes carefully between bell-to-bell bans, classroom-only restrictions, and pouch-based enforcement. Argues that effect sizes vary wildly by implementation and that 'a federal ban' is the wrong unit of analysis.
